In salicylate toxicity the net pH can be alkalemic, near-normal, or acidemic depending on the balance of the two primary processes. 2. PaCO2 is 21.4 mmHg, which is clearly low and indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. 3. If this were isolated acute respiratory alkalosis, HCO3 should be about 20.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.3-22.3), but the actual HCO3 is 17.0 mmol/L. The alkalemic pH does not rule out a concurrent metabolic acidosis when the bicarbonate is lower than acute respiratory alkalosis should produce. 4. The anion gap is 139 - (102 + 17.0) = 20.0, which is raised and proves a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis. Lactate is 2.1, so mild elevation may be present, but it is not the main explanation for the gap. 5. Glucose is 5.0 mmol/L, which is not DKA-range hyperglycaemia. 6. The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.
